Reference: Circulating Tumor Cells Exhibit Metastatic Tropism and Reveal Brain Metastasis Drivers - PubMed
Patient CTCS: Breast cancer patient-derived circulating tumor cells (CTCs)
Summary: This study uses sequencing data from breast cancer patient–derived circulating tumor cells (CTCs) and CTC-derived metastatic variants to investigate organ-specific metastasis. It demonstrates that distinct CTC populations exhibit metastatic tropism, meaning they are biologically predisposed to colonize specific organs. The study identifies molecular features associated with brain metastasis.

This study performs single-cell RNA sequencing of circulating tumor cells from breast cancer patients to identify molecular drivers of metastasis. It finds that elevated ribosomal protein expression, particularly RPL15, defines an aggressive CTC subpopulation associated with increased metastatic potential and poor clinical outcomes. The results highlight translational regulation as a key mechanism in metastasis.

Reference: Marker-free characterization of full-length transcriptomes of single live circulating tumor cells - PMC
Patient CTCS: Breast cancer CTCs from multiple subtypes (ER+/-, PR+/-, HER2+/-)
Summary: This study uses single-cell RNA sequencing to analyze 81 circulating tumor cells from multiple breast cancer subtypes without relying on traditional surface markers. It introduces a marker-free computational approach to identify CTCs and distinguish them from contaminating white blood cells. The work highlights CTC heterogeneity and improves detection of previously missed CTC populations.

Reference: RNA-Seq of Circulating Tumor Cells in Stage II-III Breast Cancer - PubMed
Patient CTCS: Breast cancer patient-derived CTCs (Stage II–III, non-metastatic)
Summary: This study analyzes RNA sequencing data from circulating tumor cells in patients with Stage II–III breast cancer. It shows that CTC gene expression profiles can differ from primary tumors, revealing molecular heterogeneity at early stages of disease. The findings suggest that biologically distinct CTC populations exist before overt metastasis.

Patient CTCS: Breast cancer patient-derived circulating tumor cells (single cells vs clusters)
Summary: This study investigates the role of circulating tumor cell (CTC) clusters in breast cancer metastasis using RNA sequencing and mouse models. It demonstrates that CTC clusters have significantly higher metastatic potential than single CTCs and are more likely to become lodged in small capillaries of distant organs. The results suggest that collective migration of tumor cells plays a critical role in metastatic spread.

Reference: Circulating Tumor Cells Exhibit Metastatic Tropism and Reveal Brain Metastasis Drivers - PubMed
Patient CTCS: Breast cancer patient-derived circulating tumor cells
Summary: This study compares single circulating tumor cells and CTC clusters using RNA sequencing. It demonstrates that CTC clusters have significantly higher metastatic potential than single cells and identifies plakoglobin as a key regulator of cluster formation. The findings suggest that collective cell migration plays a major role in breast cancer metastasis.
